analysis:
Now I have comprehensive research. Let me compose the analysis.

• Markets price near-zero chance (1-3%) of ceasefire or conflict resolution by month-end, with military action continuing through March 31 at 95% confidence. The term structure — 13% by Apr 7, 23% by Apr 15, 39% by Apr 30 — implies traders see a narrow but widening window for a deal in April, likely contingent on progress through Pakistan-mediated indirect channels after Iran rejected the US 15-point proposal on March 25.

• Today's major escalation: Houthi forces in Yemen launched their first ballistic missile at Israel, marking a new front in the month-old war. Combined with Iranian missile strikes on central Israel retaliating for nuclear site attacks and US troops injured at a Saudi air base on March 27, the conflict is broadening rather than narrowing — a trajectory inconsistent with near-term ceasefire pricing.

• The ceasefire/conflict-end spread reveals market skepticism about a comprehensive deal: ceasefire by Apr 30 trades at 39% but conflict-ends-by-Apr-30 at only 36%, suggesting traders believe even a US-Iran ceasefire may not end the broader regional war involving Hezbollah, Houthis, and Iraqi militias — consistent with Iran's demand for a "comprehensive end across all fronts."

• Kalshi's Strait of Hormuz markets align with Polymarket's timeline: odds of normal tanker traffic resuming before Apr 15 are below 25%, rising to ~67% by June 1. Oil analysts warn mid-April is the inflection point — Brent settled at $112.57/bbl on March 27, off the $126 peak but still the highest since 2022. A prolonged Hormuz closure beyond April risks global recession per Dallas Fed and Oxford Economics assessments.

• Insider trading allegations cloud market integrity: Polymarket flagged eight new accounts that placed ~$70K in bets on a March 31 ceasefire around March 21, standing to win $820K — and Kalshi banned two traders for similar activity. The high-volume ceasefire contracts ($36.2M on the March 31 market alone) are drawing scrutiny from analysts who question whether price movements reflect information asymmetry or speculative noise.

• Near-term catalysts to watch: (1) Whether Pakistan can convert indirect messaging into formal talks before Trump's extended strike-pause deadline expires around April 6 — the Apr 7 ceasefire contract at 13% suggests low confidence; (2) Iran's diminishing missile inventory (started with ~2,500, significant portion destroyed) may create pressure to negotiate before leverage erodes further; (3) Houthi escalation risks triggering US strikes on Yemen, which would widen the war and push ceasefire timelines further out. CSIS and RAND analysts assess no rapid decisive outcome is likely — air power alone cannot force regime capitulation.

Sources:
- [Al Jazeera: Iran war live updates, March 28](https://www.aljazeera.com/news/liveblog/2026/3/28/iran-war-live-trump-again-slams-natos-lack-of-support-for-war-on-tehran)
